title: Origami fox face
subtitle: Folding a simple fox head from a square sheet
excerpt: The origami fox face is a flat, two-dimensional model made in six folds from a square sheet. The ears are formed by folding two corners forward, the snout by folding one corner upward, and the nose by making a small reverse fold at the tip. The result is a recognisable fox face that works well as a bookmark, a card decoration, or a first animal fold for beginners.
sourceNotes: Traditional simple fox-face fold widely taught in beginner origami curricula. No single attributable originator; described in general origami instructional literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold in which the paper is pushed away from you, creating a ridge or peak. The paper forms a mountain shape. In origami diagrams, shown as a dash-dot line. The reverse of a valley fold: the crease faces outward, away from the viewer.

content.text: alongside valley folds. Valley folds bring paper toward you; mountain folds push it away. The fox face uses one mountain fold for the nose reverse: this is the only fold where the paper moves away from you. Everything else is a valley fold. Keeping track of which fold goes which direction is the main skill in reading origami diagrams, and the fox face is a good place to practise it.

items.name: Square origami paper, 15 cm (slug: kami-15cm)
items.description: Orange, red-brown, or tan works well for a fox. The coloured side will become the face. Plain paper in any colour is fine for practice.

items.name: Mini bone folder (slug: origami-bone-folder-mini)
items.description: Optional. Helpful for the small nose reverse fold in step 5. Finger pressure alone is sufficient for the other folds.

content.text: Place the square coloured side down, oriented as a diamond with a point at the top. Fold the bottom point up to meet the top point, creating a triangle. Press the crease flat. The coloured side is now on the inside; the fold is a valley fold that closes the square along the diagonal. This triangle is the starting point for all the following folds.

The triangle has a long horizontal base at the bottom and a point at the top. Fold the left corner of the base upward and slightly outward: aim for a position about 30-40 mm above the base line and angled outward from the centre. Press the fold flat. Repeat for the right corner, mirroring the angle. These two upward folds form the fox's ears. The ear angle determines the expression: ears angled outward appear alert; ears angled more steeply inward appear flattened.

Fold the bottom point of the triangle upward to a position roughly two-thirds of the way up the face: leave the top third of the original triangle visible as the forehead between the ears. Press the fold flat. The upward fold creates the muzzle area. The crease line is horizontal; the fold is a valley fold bringing the bottom point toward you.
Step 4: fold back the nose tip

The upward snout fold has created a small point at the top of the muzzle. Fold this small point back downward: away from you: to create the fox's nose. This is a mountain fold: the tip folds behind the muzzle rather than forward over it. The small triangle that folds back forms a dark triangular nose shape when the coloured paper is visible. Adjust the size of this fold to make the nose more prominent or more subtle.
Step 5: finishing and drawing the face

Turn the model over. The coloured face is now visible. Add eyes with a pen: two small circles positioned in the upper half of the face, below the ear bases and above the muzzle fold line. The fox face is flat and can be used as a bookmark by sliding the bottom edge (the original base of the triangle) over a page corner. It also works as a card decoration or an embellishment pasted onto a journalling page.
